title: Integration Surface Mapping
use_for: turning decompiled assembly evidence into runtime integration contracts
selecting hook, probe, snapshot, and facade candidates from game dumps
mapping unstable Unity game internals to explicit modding boundaries
bridging assembly analysis with Harmony and reflection-based runtime integration
do_not_use_for: raw assembly inventory without integration intent
rules: choose one integration concern before mapping targets
separate evidence, interpretation, contract, and runtime validation
prefer observable lifecycle anchors before mutation points
wrap private or version-sensitive state behind probe adapters
define diagnostics and fallback behavior for every fragile surface
hand off exact targets to modding skills without letting support skills override the analysis result
procedure: Select the runtime concern to map.
Collect relevant dump and metadata evidence.
Identify lifecycle anchors, state carriers, public calls, private members, and network or UI contexts.
Classify each candidate as hook, direct API, reflection probe, snapshot source, facade operation, or rejected surface.
Score candidate stability and runtime risk.
Define validation probes, diagnostics, and fallback behavior.
Emit a runtime integration contract for modding implementation.

primary_when: the request is about deriving runtime hooks, probes, snapshots, or facades from decompiled game code
the request asks how to go from dump output to a modding integration design
the request is about selecting stable targets or integration anchors from game internals
must_not_own_when: the request is only about executing already selected Harmony patches
the request is only about loader entry or plugin startup
the request is only about raw assembly dump generation

solves: the gap between reverse-engineered code and safe runtime mod integration
turning decompiled type and member knowledge into explicit hook, probe, event, snapshot, and facade contracts
preventing feature code from depending directly on unstable game internals
does_not_solve: implementing the Harmony patch class itself
choosing feature-specific gameplay behavior after the integration boundary exists

core_principles: Integration mapping is an analysis product, not patch implementation.
Evidence must stay traceable to exact assemblies, types, members, and signatures.
Lifecycle anchors are usually stronger contracts than incidental data reads.
Private fields and reflected calls are unstable surfaces and need adapter boundaries.
Every selected surface should declare confidence, fallback behavior, and diagnostics.
The final handoff should be implementable by modding skills without redoing the analysis.
constraints: Do not select a hook target without exact declaring type and signature evidence.
Do not treat a private member as safe simply because reflection can read it.
Do not leak raw game objects into feature modules when a snapshot or facade can isolate them.
Do not merge multiple runtime concerns into one broad integration surface unless the game API forces it.
Do not hide rejected candidates; record why they were rejected when they were plausible.
decision_rules: If the task starts from decompiled files and asks what to hook, use integration-surface-mapping as primary.
If exact target signatures are missing, attach metadata-parsing before modding handoff.
If the selected target is a lifecycle transition, hand off to harmony-patching with postfix-first guidance.
If the selected surface is private state, hand off a reflection probe contract with degradation logging.
If the selected surface will be consumed by feature modules, require a snapshot or facade boundary.
If candidate stability is low, require diagnostics and optional feature gating before implementation.

preferred_patterns: evidence table with exact type, member, signature, source assembly, and confidence
candidate classification into hook, direct API, reflection probe, snapshot source, facade operation, or rejected surface
thin hook plus handler plus internal event for lifecycle anchors
reflection adapter plus snapshot for private state
public facade over raw game internals for feature consumption
diagnostics snapshot for hook and probe availability
avoid_patterns: jumping directly from decompiled method text to feature code
one patch that observes, interprets, mutates, renders UI, and persists state
feature modules reading private fields through ad hoc reflection
choosing targets only because their names look familiar

recipes.name: Map a game lifecycle event from dump to runtime contract
recipes.when: Use when decompiled code reveals a method that appears to mark a runtime transition.
steps: Record the exact type, method, signature, and source assembly.
Inspect nearby methods and state writes to confirm the transition meaning.
Classify the target as postfix observation unless pre-mutation state is required.
Define the minimal payload that the hook handler should receive.
Define the internal event or lifecycle snapshot updated by the handler.
Define fallback behavior if the method is missing after a game update.

recipes.name: Design a reflection probe from decompiled private state
recipes.when: Use when the mod needs game state that has no stable public access path.
steps: Record all candidate member names, declaring types, and expected value shapes.
Prefer read-only access and avoid writes unless the mutation contract is proven.
Wrap access in a dedicated adapter with narrow methods.
Log missing members once per adapter and expose unavailable state to diagnostics.
Convert raw values into snapshots before feature modules consume them.

recipes.name: Reject a tempting integration surface
recipes.when: Use when a decompiled method or field appears useful but has poor stability or unclear semantics.
steps: Record why the candidate was considered.
Identify the failure mode: unclear lifecycle, broad update loop, private incidental field, missing signature confidence, or high mutation risk.
Find a more semantic lifecycle anchor or a narrower state carrier if possible.
Keep the rejected candidate in notes so future analysis does not rediscover it blindly.

pitfalls: Treating dump output as implementation instructions instead of evidence.
Selecting hooks from method names without checking payload, timing, and side effects.
Building feature code around raw reflected objects.
Failing to preserve the confidence level of each selected target.
Ignoring fallback behavior until after a game update breaks the mod.
Letting runtime implementation details overwrite the analysis handoff.
review_checklist: Is the integration concern narrowly named?
Does every selected surface trace back to exact assembly evidence?
Are candidates classified as hook, direct API, reflection probe, snapshot source, facade operation, or rejected?
Are lifecycle anchors preferred over incidental update-loop targets?
Are private/reflected members isolated behind adapters?
Does the handoff define diagnostics and fallback behavior?
Can modding implementation proceed without redoing assembly analysis?

references.title: Reverse Integration Map
references.interpretation: Use this first for the evidence-to-runtime-contract workflow.
references.why: It defines how assembly analysis becomes hook, probe, snapshot, and facade design.

references.title: Dump Pipeline
references.interpretation: Use when the integration map depends on repeatable dump output or version comparison.
references.why: It keeps the evidence source deterministic and diff-friendly.

references.title: Runtime Integration
references.interpretation: Use after candidate classification to shape the modding handoff.
references.why: It describes how selected surfaces become patches, probes, modules, events, diagnostics, and facades.
